body: This document exists to define how agents should model large UX features from broad product intent down to fine-grained acceptance criteria. Use it when a App Behavior corpus is too large to be represented safely as a flat list of requirements.

All hierarchy objects describe UX and product behavior only. They must not define architecture ownership, controller design, store design, code paths, raw state variables, or implementation sequencing.

content.title: Refinement Chain
body: Use this chain for large features:
The chain is progressive refinement. Higher layers explain why the experience exists and how users move through it. Lower layers define exact behavior and proof.

content.title: Feature
body: Use a feature item for a broad product area with multiple journeys, surfaces, or subfeatures.
Do not use it for a single button, modal action, field, or implementation component.
Completeness standard: a future agent should understand what the feature owns, why it matters, where users enter and leave, what success means, and what should not be absorbed into this feature.

content.title: Subfeature
body: Use a subfeature item for a coherent UX surface or workflow inside a larger feature.
Do not use it for a code folder, component boundary, or small visual detail unless it has its own user behavior surface.
Completeness standard: a future agent should know what behavior this subfeature governs and where to place new journeys, flows, and requirements.

content.title: Journey
body: Use a journey item for a user goal across one or more flows.
Do not use it for a single implementation action or a narrow validation rule.
Completeness standard: a future agent should understand the user's intent before writing lower-level behavior requirements.

content.title: Flow
body: Use a flow item for a coherent user path with a start, end, and expected outcome.
Do not use it for every tiny click target. Use subflows for narrower behavioral units.
Completeness standard: a future agent should be able to break the flow into subflows without guessing the primary path or recovery cases.

content.title: Subflow
body: Use a subflow item for a stable behavioral unit inside a flow.
Do not use it for implementation helper behavior that the user cannot observe.
Completeness standard: a future agent should be able to attach requirements, observable scenarios, and acceptance criteria to the subflow.

content.title: Requirement
body: Use a requirement item for one coherent UX behavior concept.
Do not use it to define implementation state management.
Required content is defined in [UX Requirement Item](02-ux-requirement-item.md).
Completeness standard: implementation and tests should be able to use the requirement as a hard behavior gate.

content.title: Observable Scenario
body: Use an observable scenario item for user-visible situations such as loading, empty, error, invalid, dirty, stale, unauthenticated, success, restore, or recovery states.
Do not use it for implementation timing details.
Completeness standard: a future agent should know what the user sees, what the user can and cannot do, what feedback appears, and how the scenario settles.

content.title: Acceptance Criterion
body: Use an acceptance criterion item for proof expectations tied to requirements, scenarios, or subflows.
Do not use it to duplicate the full requirement. It should make the proof concrete.
Completeness standard: a future agent should know the given/when/then behavior, proof priority, automation expectation, and what failure means.

content.title: Pending Decision
body: Use a pending decision item when product intent, behavior change, or confirmation is unresolved.
Do not hide unresolved behavior inside notes fields. Pending decisions should be first-class so they can block or route implementation safely.
